## Breadcrumb

# Desalination Basics

[ITO Water and Energy Research](/eere/iedo/water-and-energy-rdd "Water and Energy RD&D")

## What Is Desalination and Why Is It Important?

Desalination is the process of removing salts and other minerals from water, making it suitable for human consumption, irrigation, or industrial uses. To separate out these contaminants, water is either distilled through thermal desalination or pumped through a membrane that holds back impurities but allows water to pass through.

Desalination allows communities to use a broader variety of water sources than conventional techniques, improving the resilience of water-stressed communities. About 30% of water in the U.S. comes from underground water reserves known as aquifers. Much of the water in these aquifers, especially in coastal regions, is too brackish (salty) for human use. If we were able to use these reserves, the brackish groundwater available in the U.S. would satisfy nearly 800 years of use at current rates, according to a [U.S. Geological Survey report](https://pubs.er.usgs.gov/publication/pp1833).

Accessing brackish water sources will become increasingly important. As sea levels rise, existing freshwater aquifers for coastal communities are at an increasingly high risk of seawater intrusion. Desalination is an important potential tool to help these communities treat contaminated water.

## How Does Desalination Work?

Seawater, brackish groundwater, treated wastewater, agricultural runoff, and even water generated as a by-product from oil and gas extraction can all be made fit for human use through desalination.

Most treatment options to remove salts from water are either thermal desalination or membrane-based technologies, although other technologies are also under development.

Thermal desalination systems heat water so that it evaporates into steam, leaving behind impurities, which then condenses back into a liquid for human use. Yet these phase changes (from liquid to steam and back to liquid) mean that thermal desalination is more energy intensive than modern membrane technologies. Some of the thermal energy applied to evaporate the water can be recovered as the steam condenses back into liquid water, but a significant amount of heat ends up being wasted.

Large desalination facilities sometimes use technologies such as combined heat and power and waste heat reuse to achieve higher efficiencies. However, even the most efficient thermal desalination processes typically use more energy than alternatives like reverse osmosis.

Thermal desalination is the more popular—and sometimes only—option for applications that are beyond the capabilities of membranes. Thermal systems can treat water to a very high water purity (necessary for some industrial applications) and treat input water with a particularly high salt content, which membranes can't handle.

Membrane-based desalination describes a class of technologies in which saline water passes through a semipermeable material that allows water through but holds back dissolved solids like salts.

Reverse osmosis is the most common membrane technology—more than two-thirds of desalination facilities nationwide are brackish water reverse osmosis facilities, according to a report by the [U.S. Bureau of Reclamation](https://www.usbr.gov/research/dwpr/reportpdfs/report207.pdf).

Osmosis refers to the natural movement of water from an area of high salt concentration to an area of low salt concentration. Reverse osmosis uses high-energy pumps to reverse that process, pressurizing water across one side of the membrane, leaving behind highly concentrated salty brine on the other.

Saltier water requires more pressure to force the water through the membrane, which can end up rupturing the membrane. The strength of the membrane thus creates an upper limit on the salinity of water that can be treated. Therefore, water with much more salt content than seawater typically cannot be purified with reverse osmosis technologies.

The U.S. Department of Energy's [Industrial Technologies Office](/eere/ito/industrial-technologies-office "Industrial Technologies Office") is investing in research, development, and demonstration opportunities for cost-effective, energy efficient desalination technologies.

# What Is Desalination? Definition, Pros and Cons.

We are in the midst of a global crisis, with billions of people lacking safe, reliable access to a fundamental necessity of life: clean water.

The reasons behind water scarcity vary by region, but the primary factors include insufficient water infrastructure and ecological conditions. Climate change, which plays a role in lengthening and strengthening droughts and rising temperatures, only exacerbates the problem.

As drinking water becomes more scarce, finding new sources has become a top priority around the world. One promising technology to help address this challenge is desalination. Market research firm Arizton predicts the global desalination market will grow to $28.1 billion by 2026, up from $16.5 billion in 2020.

What is desalination? In the simplest terms, desalination is the process of removing salt from seawater, which offers the potential to generate billions of gallons of potable water. However, it comes with its own challenges. The process is energy-intensive, and that energy is often derived from fossil fuels, making it expensive and harmful to the environment.

But there are solutions. Knowledgeable professionals with expertise and an [advanced education in engineering](/) can help balance meeting human needs and protecting the environment.  
  




![A water resources engineer examines piping at a plant.](./media_1ae089d09b9178229f56c9acb11821fee2e3d71f0.jpg?width=750&format=jpg&optimize=medium)

![A water resources engineer examines piping at a plant.](./media_1ae089d09b9178229f56c9acb11821fee2e3d71f0.jpg?width=750&format=jpg&optimize=medium)

## Desalination Explained

The desalination process removes dissolved salts from water. This can include seawater, water from inland seas, mineralized groundwater, and municipal wastewater. The aim is to convert seawater into fresh water that’s safe for drinking, irrigation, and other uses.

Desalination is far from new. Ancient civilizations used various methods for desalination, including distillation and sedimentation. For example, in the fourth century B.C., the Greek city of Cyrene used a complex system of cisterns and filters to supply water to the city.

The widespread use of desalination originated in the mid-20th century. New technologies such as reverse osmosis, thermal distillation, and electrodialysis have made distillation more cost-effective.

In the modern era, desalination is an increasingly important solution amid the climate crisis, which has led to decreased precipitation. Desalination is helping meet the growing demand for water in many areas of the world where freshwater resources are scarce.

## How Does Desalination Work?

For engineers who want to help solve the water crisis, understanding how desalination works is critical. The process involves separating salt molecules from water molecules. Several desalination methods can accomplish this, each with a different approach. For example, membrane processes are a class of water treatment technologies that use permeable membranes to separate salts and other impurities from water as it passes through. The two main types of membrane processes are reverse osmosis and electrodialysis. Below are descriptions of several different desalination methods.

## Pros and Cons of Desalination

Desalination can increase water security, turning seawater into a dependable local source of water for drinking and irrigation. This is particularly helpful in regions with limited water resources that are facing droughts.

Desalination can also make communities less reliant on distant sources of fresh water, reducing the need to extract and transport water over long distances. In turn, this minimizes the need for heavy machinery and vehicles for transport, reducing carbon usage.

But despite what desalination can help accomplish, it also has drawbacks. For example, it can be expensive, requiring building and maintaining plants. It is also highly energy-intensive, which makes it cost-prohibitive in some regions.

Desalination processes also generate brine, a highly concentrated and salty waste product. The disposal of large volumes of brine can have negative effects on the marine environment and coastal ecosystems if not properly managed.

However, [water resources engineering](/blog/what-is-water-resources-engineering/) efforts targeting issues such as brine disposal and energy usage aim to enhance desalination processes and mitigate its damaging effects. These strategies use engineering equipment, facilities, and techniques to manage desalination processes. Water resource engineers help ensure that water is safe for humans, plants, and animals.

## Be Part of the Solution to the Water Crisis

More than 70% of the planet is covered in water, but just 2.5% of that is fresh water, of which only a small part is drinkable by humans. Desalination has already yielded positive results around the world, providing a steady source of clean drinking water to areas that have experienced droughts or natural disasters, as well as in arid regions without regular access to water. Desalinated water can also be used for irrigation and livestock, making it a valuable resource for farmers.

While drawbacks to the desalination process can include negative impacts on the environment and coastal ecosystems and heavy use of fossil fuels to run desalination plants, efforts are underway to improve the technology and minimize its negative effects. If you’re interested in gaining a better understanding of what desalination is and being part of this solution, explore the [online Master of Science in Engineering at the University of California, Riverside](/), which includes a specialization in [environmental engineering](/environmental-engineering/).

Find out how the program can help put you on the path to finding solutions to the global water crisis.

Desalination

Humans cannot drink saline water but saline water can be made into freshwater, for which there are many uses. The process is called "desalination", and it is being used more and more around the world to provide people with needed freshwater.

**•[Water Science School HOME](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topic/water-science-school)•[Water Use topics](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topic/water-science-school/science/water-use-0)•[Water Quality topics](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topic/water-science-school/science/water-quality-topics)•**

### Thirsty? How 'bout a cool, refreshing cup of seawater?

[Media ![Image 5: A floating solar still to desalinate seawater](https://d9-wret.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/assets/palladium/production/s3fs-public/styles/side_image/public/thumbnails/image/wss-desalination-solar-still.jpg?itok=1UzV3pii)](https://www.usgs.gov/media/images/a-floating-solar-still-desalinate-seawater)

 Sources/Usage: Some content may have restrictions. [View Media Details](https://www.usgs.gov/media/images/a-floating-solar-still-desalinate-seawater)

A floating solar still is used to desalinate small amounts of seawater, using evaporation and condensation.

 Credit: Landfall Navigation

No, don't take us literally! Humans cannot drink saline water. But, saline water can be made into **[freshwater](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topic/water-science-school/science/freshwater-lakes-and-rivers-and-water-cycle)**, which is the purpose of this portable, inflatable solar still (it even wraps up into a tiny package). The process is called desalination, and it is being used more and more around the world to provide people with needed freshwater. Most of the United States has, or can gain access to, ample supplies of freshwater for drinking purposes. But, freshwater can be in short supply in many parts of the Nation and world. And, as the population continues to grow, shortages of freshwater will occur more often, if only in certain locations. In some areas, **[salt water](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topic/water-science-school/science/saline-water-and-salinity)** (from the **[ocean](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topic/water-science-school/science/oceans-and-seas-and-water-cycle)**, for instance) is being turned into freshwater for drinking.

The "simple" hurdle that must be overcome to turn seawater into freshwater is to remove the dissolved salt in seawater. That may seem as easy as just boiling some seawater in a pan, capturing the steam and condensing it back into water (distillation). Other methods are available but these current technological processes must be done on a large scale to be useful to large populations, and the current processes are expensive, energy-intensive, and involve large-scale facilities.

### What makes water saline?

What do we mean by "saline water?" Water that is saline contains significant amounts (referred to as "concentrations") of dissolved salts. In this case, the concentration is the amount (by weight) of salt in water, as expressed in "parts per million" (ppm). If water has a concentration of 10,000 ppm of dissolved salts, then one percent of the weight of the water comes from dissolved salts.

Here are our parameters for saline water:

*   Freshwater - Less than 1,000 ppm
*   Slightly saline water - From 1,000 ppm to 3,000 ppm
*   Moderately saline water - From 3,000 ppm to 10,000 ppm
*   Highly saline water - From 10,000 ppm to 35,000 ppm

By the way, ocean water contains about 35,000 ppm of salt.

### The worldwide need for freshwater

The scarcity of freshwater resources and the need for additional water supplies is already critical in many arid regions of the world and will be increasingly important in the future. Many arid areas simply do not have freshwater resources in the form of surface water such as rivers and lakes. They may have only limited **[underground water resources](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topic/water-science-school/science/groundwater-what-groundwater)**, some that are becoming more brackish as extraction of water from the aquifers continues. Solar desalination evaporation is used by nature to produce **[rain](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topic/water-science-school/science/rain-and-precipitation)**, which is the main source of freshwater on earth.

According to the International Desalination Association, globally, more than 300 million people receive their water from de-salination plants. The largest desalination plant in North America, the Claude "Bud" Lewis Carlsbad Desalination Plant, can be found near San Diego, California. It creates about 10 percent of the freshwater used by the approximately 3.1 million people in the region and it costs about twice as much as other water sources. At this moment there exists 11 desalination plants in California and 10 more plants have been proposed. The largest desalination plant in the world, Sorek Desalination Plant, is located south of Tel Aviv, Israel and produces over 137 million gallons of potable water a day. In contrast, the Carlsbad plant produces approximately 50 million gallons of water a day.

In 2015, in the U.S. in some States, public supply water sources include desalinated seawater or brackish groundwater that has been treated to reduce dissolved solids. A combined total of 7.21 millions of gallons per day of saline surface water withdrawals for public supply use were reported for the U.S. Virgin Islands, Texas, Florida, and Massachusetts. A combined total of 263 millions of gallons per day of saline groundwater withdrawals for public-supply use were identified for Florida, California, Texas, Virginia, Kansas, and Utah. ([Deiter et al., 2018](https://pubs.er.usgs.gov/publication/cir1441)).

### Another method: Reverse osmosis

Another way saline water is desalinized is by the "reverse osmosis" procedure. In most simplistic terms, water, containing dissolved salt molecules, is forced through a semi-permeable membrane (essentially a filter), in which the larger salt molecules do not get through the membrane holes but the smaller water molecules do.

Reverse osmosis is an effective means to desalinate saline water, but it is more expensive than other methods. As prices come down in the future the use of reverse osmosis plants to desalinate large amounts of saline water should become more common.

### Uses for desalinated water

When we think of desalination we tend to focus on having fresh water to drink but it is important to note that desalinated seawater is not only produced for domestic/municipal use. Desalination provides freshwater for agricultural irrigation and is often used for processes that require very high water quality like that used in boilers to avoid scale buildup. Other examples include the manufacture of pharmaceuticals, semi-conductors, and hard disk drives.

### Desalination is not modern science

Distillation desalination is one of mankind's earliest forms of water treatment, and it is still a popular treatment solution throughout the world today. In ancient times, many civilizations used this process on their ships to convert sea water into drinking water. Today, desalination plants are used to convert sea water to drinking water on ships and in many arid regions of the world, and to treat water in other areas that is fouled by natural and unnatural contaminants. Distillation is perhaps the one water treatment technology that most completely reduces the widest range of drinking water contaminants.

In nature, this basic process is responsible for the**[water (hydrologic) cycle](https://www.usgs.gov/media/images/water-cycle-poster-natural-water-cycle)**. The sun supplies energy that causes water to evaporate from surface sources such as lakes, oceans, and streams. The **[water vapor](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topic/water-science-school/science/vapor-pressure-and-water)** eventually comes in contact with cooler air, where it re-condenses to form dew or rain. This process can be imitated artificially and more rapidly than in nature, using alternative sources of heating and cooling.

### Why don't we see more desalination plants if freshwater is so scarce?

The primary reason we don't see more desalination plants is due to the cost to operate these plants. Salt dissolves easily in water and it requires a lot of energy to break the chemical bonds. Energy consumption hikes up the cost to run a plant and the cost of the technology to desalinate the water also holds a hefty price tag.

Sometimes environmental protection is a factor that that can keep desalination plants from being built. In 2022, after a 20-year plan to build a desalination plant at Huntington Beach in California, the California Coastal Commission declined the bid to build the plant due to concerns about higher water rates, marine life loss, and impacts to poor households already living near industrial areas. Residents in the area did not like that the plant would have taken 100 million daily gallons of seawater, desalted half of it, and discharged the other half back as saltier brine.

### Your own personal desalination plant

Remember looking at the picture at the top of this page of a floating solar still? The same process that drives that device can also be applied if you find yourself in the desert in need of a drink of water.

The low-tech approach to accomplish this is to construct a "solar still" which uses heat from the sun to run a distillation process to cause dew to form on something like plastic sheeting. The diagram to the right illustrates this. Using seawater or plant material in the body of the distiller creates humid air, which, because of the enclosure created by the plastic sheet, is warmed by the sun. The humid air condenses water droplets on the underside of the plastic sheet, and because of**[surface tension](https://www.usgs.gov/special-topic/water-science-school/science/surface-tension-and-water)**, the water drops stick to the sheet and move downward into a trough, from which it can be consumed.

**Some desalination facts**

*   It is estimated that some 30% of the world's irrigated areas suffer from salinity problems and remediation is seen to be very costly.
*   According to the International Desalination Association, in June 2015, 18,426 desalination plants operated worldwide, producing 86.8 million cubic meters per day, providing water for 300 million people. This number increased from 78.4 million cubic meters in 2013, a 10.71% increase in 2 years.
*   The most important users of desalinated water are in the Middle East, (mainly Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar and Bahrain), which uses about 70% of worldwide capacity; and in North Africa (mainly Libya and Algeria), which uses about 6% of worldwide capacity.
*   Among industrialized countries, the United States is one of the most important users of desalinated water, especially in California and parts of Florida. The cost of desalination has kept desalination from being used more often.
*   In 2015, in California, about 11% of total water use withdrawals were from saline surface water and saline groundwater.

## Contents

# Desalination

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Reverse_osmosis_desalination_plant.JPG/250px-Reverse_osmosis_desalination_plant.JPG)

**Desalination** is a process that removes mineral components from [saline water](/wiki/Saline_water "Saline water"). More generally, desalination is the removal of salts and minerals from a substance.[[1]](#cite_note-1) One example is [soil desalination](/wiki/Soil_salinity_control "Soil salinity control"). This is important for agriculture. It is possible to desalinate saltwater, especially [sea water](/wiki/Seawater "Seawater"), to produce water for human consumption or irrigation, producing [brine](/wiki/Brine "Brine") as a by-product.[[2]](#cite_note-PanagopoulosHaralambousLoizidou2019-2) Many seagoing ships and [submarines](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine") use desalination. Modern interest in desalination mostly focuses on cost-effective provision of [fresh water](/wiki/Fresh_water "Fresh water") for human use. Along with recycled [wastewater](/wiki/Wastewater "Wastewater"), it is one of the few [water resources](/wiki/Water_resources "Water resources") independent of rainfall.[[3]](#cite_note-Fischetti2007-3)

Due to its energy consumption, desalinating sea water is generally more costly than fresh water from [surface water](/wiki/Surface_water "Surface water") or [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater "Groundwater"), [water recycling](/wiki/Reclaimed_water "Reclaimed water") and [water conservation](/wiki/Water_conservation "Water conservation"); however, these alternatives are not always available and depletion of reserves is a critical problem worldwide.[[4]](#cite_note-EbrahimiNajafpourYousefiKebria2019-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Transcript-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Desalination processes are using either thermal methods (in the case of [distillation](/wiki/Distillation "Distillation")) or membrane-based methods (e.g. in the case of [reverse osmosis](/wiki/Reverse_osmosis "Reverse osmosis")).[[7]](#cite_note-Cohen_2021_p.-7)[[8]](#cite_note-IWA2022-8): 24

An estimate in 2018 found that "18,426 desalination plants are in operation in over 150 countries. They produce 87 million cubic meters of clean water each day and supply over 300 million people."[[8]](#cite_note-IWA2022-8): 24 The energy intensity has improved: It is now about 3 kWh/m3 (in 2018), down by a factor of 10 from 20–30 kWh/m3 in 1970.[[8]](#cite_note-IWA2022-8): 24 Nevertheless, desalination represented about 25% of the energy consumed by the [water sector](/wiki/Water_sector "Water sector") in 2016.[[8]](#cite_note-IWA2022-8): 24

## History

Ancient Greek philosopher [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") observed in his work *[Meteorology](/wiki/Meteorology_(Aristotle) "Meteorology (Aristotle)")* that "salt water, when it turns into vapour, becomes sweet and the vapour does not form salt water again when it condenses", and that a fine wax vessel would hold potable water after being submerged long enough in seawater, having acted as a membrane to filter the salt.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

At the same time the desalination of seawater was recorded in China. Both the *Classic of Mountains and Water Seas* in the [Period of the Warring States](/wiki/Period_of_the_Warring_States "Period of the Warring States") and the *Theory of the Same Year* in the [Eastern Han Dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") mentioned that people found that the bamboo mats used for steaming rice would form a thin outer layer after long use. The as-formed thin film had [adsorption](/wiki/Adsorption "Adsorption") and [ion exchange](/wiki/Ion_exchange "Ion exchange") functions, which could adsorb salt.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Numerous examples of experimentation in desalination appeared throughout Antiquity and the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) but desalination became feasible on a large scale only in the modern era.[[12]](#cite_note-12) A good example of this experimentation comes from [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci") (Florence, 1452), who realized that distilled water could be made cheaply in large quantities by adapting a [still](/wiki/Still "Still") to a cookstove.[[13]](#cite_note-13) During the Middle Ages elsewhere in Central Europe, work continued on distillation refinements, although not necessarily directed towards desalination.[[14]](#cite_note-Birkett1984-14)

The first major land-based desalination plant may have been installed under emergency conditions on an island off the coast of [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia") in 1560.[[14]](#cite_note-Birkett1984-14)[[15]](#cite_note-NebbiaMenozzi1966-15) It is believed that a garrison of 700 Spanish soldiers was besieged by the Turkish army and that, during the siege, the captain in charge fabricated a [still](/wiki/Still "Still") capable of producing 40 barrels of fresh water per day, though details of the device have not been reported.[[15]](#cite_note-NebbiaMenozzi1966-15)

Before the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), desalination was primarily of concern to oceangoing ships, which otherwise needed to keep on board supplies of fresh water. Sir [Richard Hawkins](/wiki/Richard_Hawkins "Richard Hawkins") (1562–1622), who made extensive travels in the [South Seas](/wiki/South_Seas "South Seas"), reported that he had been able to supply his men with fresh water by means of shipboard distillation.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Additionally, during the early 1600s, several prominent figures of the era such as [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon "Francis Bacon") and [Walter Raleigh](/wiki/Walter_Raleigh "Walter Raleigh") published reports on desalination.[[15]](#cite_note-NebbiaMenozzi1966-15)[[17]](#cite_note-17) These reports and others,[[18]](#cite_note-18) set the climate for the first patent dispute concerning desalination apparatus. The two first patents regarding water desalination were approved in 1675 and 1683 (patents No. 184[[19]](#cite_note-19) and No. 226,[[20]](#cite_note-20) published by [William Walcot](/wiki/William_Walcot "William Walcot") and Robert Fitzgerald (and others), respectively). Nevertheless, neither of the two inventions entered service as a consequence of scale-up difficulties.[[14]](#cite_note-Birkett1984-14) No significant improvements to the basic seawater distillation process were made during the 150 years from the mid-1600s until 1800.

When the frigate *[Protector](/wiki/Protector_(1779_frigate) "Protector (1779 frigate)")* was sold to Denmark in the 1780s (as the ship *Hussaren*) its still was studied and recorded in great detail.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In the United States, [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") catalogued heat-based methods going back to the 1500s, and formulated practical advice that was publicized to all U.S. ships on the reverse side of sailing clearance permits.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Beginning about 1800, things started changing as a consequence of the appearance of the [steam engine](/wiki/Steam_engine "Steam engine") and the so-called [age of steam](/wiki/Steam_power_during_the_Industrial_Revolution "Steam power during the Industrial Revolution").[[14]](#cite_note-Birkett1984-14) Knowledge of the thermodynamics of steam processes[[24]](#cite_note-24) and the need for a pure water source for its use in boilers[[25]](#cite_note-25) generated a positive effect regarding distilling systems. Additionally, the spread of [European colonialism](/wiki/European_colonialism "European colonialism") induced a need for freshwater in remote parts of the world, thus creating the appropriate climate for water desalination.[[14]](#cite_note-Birkett1984-14)

In parallel with the development and improvement of systems using steam ([multiple-effect evaporators](/wiki/Multiple-effect_evaporator "Multiple-effect evaporator")), these types of devices quickly demonstrated their desalination potential.[[14]](#cite_note-Birkett1984-14) In 1852, [Alphonse René le Mire de Normandy](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Normandy,_Alphonse_Ren%C3%A9_Le_Mire_de "wikisource:Normandy, Alphonse René Le Mire de") was issued a British patent for a vertical tube seawater distilling unit that, thanks to its simplicity of design and ease of construction, gained popularity for shipboard use.[[14]](#cite_note-Birkett1984-14) Land-based units did not significantly appear until the latter half of the nineteenth century.[[26]](#cite_note-Birkett2010-26) In the 1860s, the US Army purchased three Normandy evaporators, each rated at 7000 gallons/day and installed them on the islands of [Key West](/wiki/Key_West "Key West") and [Dry Tortugas](/wiki/Dry_Tortugas "Dry Tortugas").[[14]](#cite_note-Birkett1984-14)[[26]](#cite_note-Birkett2010-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) Another land-based plant was installed at [Suakin](/wiki/Suakin "Suakin") during the 1880s that provided freshwater to the British troops there. It consisted of six-effect distillers with a capacity of 350 tons/day.[[14]](#cite_note-Birkett1984-14)[[26]](#cite_note-Birkett2010-26)

After World War II, many technologies were developed or improved such as Multi Effect Flash desalination (MEF) and Multi Stage Flash desalination (MSF). Another notable technology is freeze-thaw desalination.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Freeze-thaw desalination, (cryo-desalination or FD), excludes dissolved minerals from saline water through crystallization.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The Office of Saline Water was created in the [United States Department of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Interior "United States Department of the Interior") in 1955 in accordance with the Saline Water Conversion Act of 1952.[[5]](#cite_note-Transcript-5)[[30]](#cite_note-Records-30) This act was motivated by a water shortage in California and inland western United States. The Department of the Interior allocated resources including research grants, expert personnel, patent data, and land for experiments to further advancements.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The results of these efforts included the construction of over 200 electrodialysis and distillation plants globally, [reverse osmosis](/wiki/Reverse_osmosis "Reverse osmosis") (RO) research, and international cooperation (for example, the First International Water Desalination Symposium and Exposition in 1965).[[32]](#cite_note-32) The Office of Saline Water merged into the Office of Water Resources Research in 1974.[[30]](#cite_note-Records-30)

The first industrial desalination plant in the United States opened in [Freeport, Texas](/wiki/Freeport,_Texas "Freeport, Texas") in 1961 after a decade of regional drought.[[5]](#cite_note-Transcript-5)

By the late 1960s and the early 1970s, RO started to show promising results to replace traditional thermal desalination units. Research took place at state universities in California, at the [Dow Chemical Company](/wiki/Dow_Chemical_Company "Dow Chemical Company") and [DuPont](/wiki/DuPont "DuPont").[[33]](#cite_note-33) Many studies focus on ways to optimize desalination systems.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) The first commercial RO plant, the Coalinga desalination plant, was inaugurated in California in 1965 for [brackish water](/wiki/Brackish_water "Brackish water").[[36]](#cite_note-36) [Dr. Sidney Loeb](/wiki/Sidney_Loeb "Sidney Loeb"), in conjunction with staff at [UCLA](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles"), designed a large pilot plant to gather data on RO, but was successful enough to provide freshwater to the residents of Coalinga. This was a milestone in desalination technology, as it proved the feasibility of RO and its advantages compared to existing technologies (efficiency, no phase change required, ambient temperature operation, scalability, and ease of standardization).[[37]](#cite_note-37) A few years later, in 1975, the first [sea water](/wiki/Sea_water "Sea water") reverse osmosis desalination plant came into operation.

As of 2000, more than 2000 plants were operated. The largest are in Saudi Arabia, Israel, and the UAE; and the biggest plant with a volume of 1,401,000 m3/d is in Saudi Arabia (Ras Al Khair).[[38]](#cite_note-AngelakisValipourChooAhmedBabaKumarToorWang2021-38)

This decade also saw progress in integrating renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, into desalination systems. Though initially in early stages, these efforts paved the way for more environmentally sustainable desalination practices.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The 2010s and 2020s marked the emergence of next-generation membranes, including graphene-based membranes, aquaporin-inspired biomimetic membranes, ceramic membranes, and nanocomposites. These materials significantly improved water permeability, selectivity, and fouling resistance.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

As of 2021 22,000 plants were in operation[[38]](#cite_note-AngelakisValipourChooAhmedBabaKumarToorWang2021-38) In 2024 the Catalan government installed a floating offshore plant near the port of Barcelona and purchased 12 mobile desalination units for the northern region of the Costa Brava to combat the severe drought.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In 2012, cost averaged $0.75 per cubic meter. By 2022, that had declined (before inflation) to $0.41. Desalinated supplies are growing at a 10%+ compound rate, doubling in abundance every seven years.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Between 2024 and 2025, Spain has recently announced a €340 million investment to build Africa’s largest desalination plant in Casablanca, demonstrating the growing importance of large-scale desalination infrastructure.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Applications

| External audio | |
| --- | --- |
| audio icon ["Making the Deserts Bloom: Harnessing nature to deliver us from drought"](https://www.sciencehistory.org/distillations/podcast/making-the-deserts-bloom), Distillations Podcast and transcript, Episode 239, March 19, 2019, [Science History Institute](/wiki/Science_History_Institute "Science History Institute") | |

![audio icon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Nuvola_apps_arts.svg/20px-Nuvola_apps_arts.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Multiflash.svg/500px-Multiflash.svg.png)

There are now about 21,000 desalination plants in operation around the globe. The biggest ones are in the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates"), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia"), and [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"). The world's largest desalination plant is located in [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") ([Ras Al-Khair Power and Desalination Plant](/wiki/Ras_Al-Khair_Power_and_Desalination_Plant "Ras Al-Khair Power and Desalination Plant")) with a capacity of 1,401,000 cubic meters per day.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Desalination is currently expensive compared to most alternative sources of water, and only a very small fraction of total human use is satisfied by desalination.[[45]](#cite_note-45) It is usually only economically practical for high-valued uses (such as household and industrial uses) in [arid](/wiki/Arid "Arid") areas. However, there is growth in desalination for agricultural use and highly populated areas such as Singapore[[46]](#cite_note-46) or California.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) The most extensive use is in the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf").[[49]](#cite_note-49)

While noting costs are falling, and generally positive about the technology for affluent areas in proximity to oceans, a 2005 study argued, "Desalinated water may be a solution for some water-stress regions, but not for places that are poor, deep in the interior of a continent, or at high elevation. Unfortunately, that includes some of the places with the biggest water problems.", and, "Indeed, one needs to lift the water by 2000 m, or transport it over more than 1600 km to get transport costs equal to the desalination costs."[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Thus, it may be more economical to transport fresh water from somewhere else than to desalinate it. In places far from the sea, like New Delhi, or in high places, like [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"), transport costs could match desalination costs. Desalinated water is also expensive in places that are both somewhat far from the sea and somewhat high, such as [Riyadh](/wiki/Riyadh "Riyadh") and [Harare](/wiki/Harare "Harare"). By contrast in other locations transport costs are much less, such as Beijing, [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"), [Zaragoza](/wiki/Zaragoza "Zaragoza"), [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), and, of course, coastal cities like [Tripoli](/wiki/Tripoli,_Libya "Tripoli, Libya").[[51]](#cite_note-51) After desalination at [Jubail](/wiki/Jubail "Jubail"), Saudi Arabia, water is pumped 320 km inland to [Riyadh](/wiki/Riyadh "Riyadh").[[52]](#cite_note-52) For coastal cities, desalination is increasingly viewed as a competitive choice.

In 2023, Israel was using desalination to replenish the [Sea of Galilee](/wiki/Sea_of_Galilee "Sea of Galilee")'s water supply.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Not everyone is convinced that desalination is or will be economically viable or environmentally sustainable for the foreseeable future. [Debbie Cook](/wiki/Debbie_Cook "Debbie Cook") wrote in 2011 that desalination plants can be energy intensive and costly. Therefore, water-stressed regions might do better to focus on conservation or other water supply solutions than invest in desalination plants.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

## Technologies

| Methods |

Desalination is an artificial process by which saline water (generally [sea water](/wiki/Sea_water "Sea water")) is converted to fresh water. The most common desalination processes are [distillation](/wiki/Distillation "Distillation") and [reverse osmosis](/wiki/Reverse_osmosis "Reverse osmosis").[[55]](#cite_note-55)

There are several methods.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Each has advantages and disadvantages but all are useful. The methods can be divided into membrane-based (e.g., [reverse osmosis](/wiki/Reverse_osmosis "Reverse osmosis")) and thermal-based (e.g., [multistage flash distillation](/wiki/Multi-stage_flash_distillation "Multi-stage flash distillation")) methods.[[2]](#cite_note-PanagopoulosHaralambousLoizidou2019-2) The traditional process of desalination is [distillation](/wiki/Distillation "Distillation") (i.e., boiling and re-[condensation](/wiki/Condensation "Condensation") of [seawater](/wiki/Seawater "Seawater") to leave salt and impurities behind).[[57]](#cite_note-57)

There are currently two technologies with a large majority of the world's desalination capacity: [multi-stage flash distillation](/wiki/Multi-stage_flash_distillation "Multi-stage flash distillation") and [reverse osmosis](/wiki/Reverse_osmosis "Reverse osmosis").

### Distillation

#### Solar distillation

[Solar distillation](/wiki/Solar_distillation "Solar distillation") mimics the natural water cycle, in which the sun heats sea water enough for evaporation to occur.[[58]](#cite_note-Khawaji-58) After evaporation, the water vapor is condensed onto a cool surface.[[58]](#cite_note-Khawaji-58) There are two types of solar desalination. The first type uses photovoltaic cells to convert solar energy to electrical energy to power desalination. The second type converts solar energy to heat, and is known as solar thermal powered desalination.

#### Natural evaporation

Water can evaporate through several other physical effects besides [solar irradiation](/wiki/Solar_irradiation "Solar irradiation"). These effects have been included in a multidisciplinary desalination methodology in the [IBTS Greenhouse](/wiki/IBTS_Greenhouse "IBTS Greenhouse"). The IBTS is an industrial desalination (power)plant on one side and a greenhouse operating with the natural water cycle (scaled down 1:10) on the other side. The various processes of evaporation and condensation are hosted in low-tech utilities, partly underground and the architectural shape of the building itself. This integrated biotectural system is most suitable for large scale [desert greening](/wiki/Desert_greening "Desert greening") as it has a km2 footprint for the water distillation and the same for landscape transformation in desert greening, respectively the regeneration of natural fresh water cycles.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Vacuum distillation

In [vacuum distillation](/wiki/Vacuum_distillation "Vacuum distillation") atmospheric pressure is reduced, thus lowering the temperature required to evaporate the water. Liquids boil when the [vapor pressure](/wiki/Vapor_pressure "Vapor pressure") equals the ambient pressure and vapor pressure increases with temperature. Effectively, liquids boil at a lower temperature, when the ambient atmospheric pressure is less than usual atmospheric pressure. Thus, because of the reduced pressure, low-temperature "waste" heat from electrical power generation or industrial processes can be employed.

#### Multi-stage flash distillation

Water is evaporated and separated from sea water through [multi-stage flash distillation](/wiki/Multi-stage_flash_distillation "Multi-stage flash distillation"), which is a series of [flash evaporations](/wiki/Flash_evaporation "Flash evaporation").[[58]](#cite_note-Khawaji-58) Each subsequent flash process uses energy released from the condensation of the water vapor from the previous step.[[58]](#cite_note-Khawaji-58)

#### Multiple-effect distillation

[Multiple-effect distillation](/wiki/Multiple-effect_distillation "Multiple-effect distillation") (MED) works through a series of steps called "effects".[[58]](#cite_note-Khawaji-58) Incoming water is sprayed onto pipes which are then heated to generate steam. The steam is then used to heat the next batch of incoming sea water.[[58]](#cite_note-Khawaji-58) To increase efficiency, the steam used to heat the sea water can be taken from nearby power plants.[[58]](#cite_note-Khawaji-58) Although this method is the most thermodynamically efficient among methods powered by heat,[[59]](#cite_note-WarsingerEntropy-59) a few limitations exist such as a max temperature and max number of effects.[[60]](#cite_note-Al-Shammiri-60)

#### Vapor-compression distillation

[Vapor-compression evaporation](/wiki/Vapor-compression_evaporation "Vapor-compression evaporation") involves using either a mechanical compressor or a jet stream to compress the vapor present above the liquid.[[59]](#cite_note-WarsingerEntropy-59) The compressed vapor is then used to provide the heat needed for the evaporation of the rest of the sea water.[[58]](#cite_note-Khawaji-58) Since this system only requires power, it is more cost effective if kept at a small scale.[[58]](#cite_note-Khawaji-58)

#### Membrane distillation

[Membrane distillation](/wiki/Membrane_distillation "Membrane distillation") uses a temperature difference across a membrane to evaporate vapor from a brine solution and condense pure water on the colder side.[[61]](#cite_note-WarsingerFramework-61) The design of the membrane can have a significant effect on efficiency and durability. A study found that a membrane created via co-axial [electrospinning](/wiki/Electrospinning "Electrospinning") of [PVDF](/wiki/Polyvinylidene_fluoride "Polyvinylidene fluoride")-[HFP](/wiki/Hexafluoropropylene "Hexafluoropropylene") and [silica aerogel](/wiki/Aerogel "Aerogel") was able to filter 99.99% of salt after continuous 30-day usage.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Osmosis

#### Reverse osmosis

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/PlantaSchemaNotional.png/500px-PlantaSchemaNotional.png)

The leading process for desalination in terms of installed capacity and yearly growth is [reverse osmosis](/wiki/Reverse_osmosis "Reverse osmosis") (RO).[[64]](#cite_note-64) The RO membrane processes use semipermeable membranes and applied pressure (on the membrane feed side) to preferentially induce water permeation through the membrane while rejecting salts. [Reverse osmosis plant](/wiki/Reverse_osmosis_plant "Reverse osmosis plant") membrane systems typically use less energy than thermal desalination processes.[[59]](#cite_note-WarsingerEntropy-59) Energy cost in desalination processes varies considerably depending on water salinity, plant size and process type. At present the cost of seawater desalination, for example, is higher than traditional water sources, but it is expected that costs will continue to decrease with technology improvements that include, but are not limited to, improved efficiency,[[65]](#cite_note-WarsingerBatch-65) reduction in plant footprint, improvements to plant operation and optimization, more effective feed pretreatment, and lower cost energy sources.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Reverse osmosis uses a thin-film composite membrane, which comprises an ultra-thin, aromatic polyamide thin-film. This polyamide film gives the membrane its transport properties, whereas the remainder of the thin-film composite membrane provides mechanical support. The polyamide film is a dense, void-free polymer with a high surface area, allowing for its high water permeability.[[67]](#cite_note-67) A 2021 study found that the water permeability is primarily governed by the internal nanoscale mass distribution of the polyamide active layer.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

The reverse osmosis process requires maintenance. Various factors interfere with efficiency: ionic contamination (calcium, magnesium etc.); [dissolved organic carbon](/wiki/Dissolved_organic_carbon "Dissolved organic carbon") (DOC); bacteria; viruses; [colloids](/wiki/Colloid "Colloid") and insoluble particulates; [biofouling](/wiki/Biofouling "Biofouling") and [scaling](/wiki/Fouling "Fouling"), and membrane destruction in extreme cases. To mitigate damage, various pretreatment stages are introduced. Anti-scaling inhibitors include acids and other agents such as the organic polymers [polyacrylamide](/wiki/Polyacrylamide "Polyacrylamide") and [polymaleic acid](/w/index.php?title=Polymaleic_acid&action=edit&redlink=1 "Polymaleic acid (page does not exist)"), [phosphonates](/wiki/Phosphonate "Phosphonate") and [polyphosphates](/wiki/Polyphosphate "Polyphosphate"). Inhibitors for fouling are [biocides](/wiki/Biocide "Biocide") (as oxidants against bacteria and viruses), such as chlorine, ozone, sodium or calcium hypochlorite. At regular intervals, depending on the membrane contamination; fluctuating seawater conditions; or when prompted by monitoring processes, the membranes need to be cleaned, known as emergency or shock-flushing. Flushing is done with inhibitors in a fresh water solution and the system must go offline. This procedure is environmentally risky, since contaminated water is diverted into the ocean without treatment. Sensitive [marine habitats](/wiki/Marine_habitats "Marine habitats") can be irreversibly damaged.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Off-grid [solar-powered desalination units](/wiki/Solar-powered_desalination_unit "Solar-powered desalination unit") use solar energy to fill a buffer tank on a hill with seawater.[[71]](#cite_note-71) The reverse osmosis process receives its pressurized seawater feed in non-sunlight hours by gravity, resulting in sustainable drinking water production without the need for fossil fuels, an electricity grid or batteries.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) Nano-tubes are also used for the same function (i.e., Reverse Osmosis).

Deep sea reverse osmosis (DSRO) installs equipment on the [seabed](/wiki/Seabed "Seabed") to force water through RO membranes using the ocean's naturally occurring water pressure.[[75]](#cite_note-:0-75) A 2021 study suggested DSRO could improve energy efficiency compared to standard RO by up to 50%.[[76]](#cite_note-76) The concept of DSRO has long been known, but has only recently become feasible due to technological advances from the deep sea oil and gas industry, drawing early-stage investments in DSRO startups.[[75]](#cite_note

Adaptation Option

# Desalinisation

## Description

Desalination is the process of removing salt from sea or brackish water to make it useable for a range of 'fit for use' purposes including drinking. It may thus contribute to adaptation to climate change in all those circumstances in which water scarcity occurs severely and can be exacerbated in the future, also due to climate change. However, desalination is an energy intensive process; to avoid maladaptation it is essential that desalination is done using renewable energy. Moreover, desalination produces a by-product, brine (a concentrated salt solution) that must be properly disposed of to avoid adverse impacts on the marine environment. So desalination should only be applied if other more environmentally sustainable options (e.g. [Water restrictions and water rationing](https://climate-adapt.eea.europa.eu/metadata/adaptation-options/water-restrictions-and-consumption-cuts), [Water reuse](https://climate-adapt.eea.europa.eu/metadata/adaptation-options/water-recycling)) are not available or cannot be implemented. 

Desalination techniques include: 

There are currently around 16,000 desalination plants worldwide, with a total global operating capacity of roughly 95.37 million m3/day and brine production of 141.5 million m3/day. Currently, desalination is largely used in the Middle East and North Africa (70% of global capacity), in the US, increasingly in Asia, and only to a limited extent in Europe (about 10% of global capacity). Several southern EU countries are however using desalination to help cover freshwater needs ([Jones et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0048969718349167)). 

In the EU, a small fraction of freshwater is obtained through seawater desalination. EU facilities can supply up to 2.89 billion m3 of desalted water a year (active capacity). 71% of the water produced is used for public water supply (2 billion m3, 4.2% of total water employed in public supply). 17% of the desalinated water produced in the EU is used for industrial applications, 4% in power plants, and 8% for irrigation. EU desalination plants are mainly located in Mediterranean countries, where they will be mostly needed in the future: about 1,200 plants provide a capacity of 2.37 billion m3 (82% of total EU desalination capacity) ([Magagna et al, 2019](https://publications.jrc.ec.europa.eu/repository/handle/JRC115853)).

## Adaptation Details

##### IPCC categories

##### Stakeholder participation

According to the EU legislation, in the absence of mandatory EIA there is no formal consultation process for the construction of a desalination plant. At the level of countries, stakeholder engagement in desalination projects can be required by the specific national legislation in place or activated through informal processes, e.g. to co-identify the best location of a plant.

##### Success and limiting factors

Desalination is still the most energy-intensive water treatment method and to avoid maladaptation it needs to be combined with the use of renewable sources of energy and increasing efficiency in energy use. 

The electricity requirements vary according to the desalination technology, the salinity of the water source and the desired level of purity of the desalted water at the end of the treatment. In general, membrane desalination technologies such as reverse osmosis (RO) have lower energy requirements than thermal technologies such as multi stage flash (MSF). MSF systems require roughly 83-84 kWh/m3 of energy, while large scale RO systems require 3-5 kWh/ m3 for saline water and 0.5-2.6 kWh/m3 for brackish water (Olsson, 2012 in [Magagna et al, 2019](https://publications.jrc.ec.europa.eu/repository/handle/JRC115853)). As a result, operating costs are high. The International Energy Agency has estimated that at global level, the energy consumption of desalination is expected to increase eight-fold by 2040, due to increased demand for freshwater ([International Energy Agency, 2016](https://www.iea.org/reports/water-energy-nexus)). 

Research is focusing on increasing the energy efficiency of the desalination process and on increasing the use of clean energy. Practices combining desalination with renewable energies include: 

Brine discharge can negatively impact on local marine ecosystems as it increases salinity levels in seawater. Brine produced by the desalination process contains chemicals used during the pre-treatment phase. As brine is heavier than normal seawater, it accumulates on the seafloor, threatening species which are sensitive to the level of salinity. ([EEA, 2012](https://www.eea.europa.eu/publications/towards-efficient-use-of-water)). Research is investigating the best way to solve or minimise environmental problems caused by brine discharge and management. For example, the LIFE [ZELDA](http://zelda.ctm.com.es/en) project demonstrated the technical and economic feasibility of brine management strategies based on the use of electrodialysis metathesis (EDM) and valuable compound recovery processes with the final aim of reaching a zero liquid discharge (ZLD) process. Brine can also be converted to chemicals that can be re-used in the desalination process itself ([Kumar et al., 2019](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41929-018-0218-y)). 

##### Costs and benefits

The main drivers of costs are the used technology, energy cost, plant size and configuration, quality of feedwater and of desalinised water, and environmental compliance requirements. Most of these factors are site-specific in nature. Costs of conveyance and distribution of water are also important, and there are cost advantages for plants located near the coast and on low-lying land (due to lower energy needs for transport upwards; a 100-meter vertical lift is about as costly as a 100-kilometer horizontal transport). 

Overall, thermal desalination technologies, particularly MSF plants, are more capital-intensive than SWRO. However maintaining and operating costs for SWRO plants for each unit of output are double than those of MSF plants, and three times than those of MED plants. For both technologies, but particularly for thermal plants, energy is far and away the largest single item of recurrent cost. The quality of the source water (such as salinity, temperature, and biofouling elements) affects costs, performance, and durability, but also the water quality that can be achieved through the desalination process. 

##### Legal aspects

The Communication "Addressing the challenge of water scarcity and droughts in the European Union" in 2007 and later the Blueprint to Safeguard Europe’s water resources (2012) propose a hierarchy of water measures, considering that alternative water supply through desalination should be used as a last resort once other improvement of efficiency in demand and production have been exhausted. The communication on resource efficiency (COM(2011) 21), aims to create a framework for policies to support the shift towards a resource-efficient and low-carbon economy. Desalination is mentioned as an option that provides a solution to water supply problems but it may increase fossil fuel consumption and greenhouse gas emissions, if it is not powered with renewable energy. The EU aims to be climate-neutral by 2050 – an economy with net-zero greenhouse gas emissions. This objective is at the heart of the European Green Deal and in line with the EU’s commitment to global climate action under the Paris Agreement. This will require a shift towards renewable energy driven desalination plants with a higher energy efficiency.  

##### Implementation time

Implementation time of desalination plants typically ranges between 3 and 6 years including all phases from planning to operational. 

##### Lifetime

The lifetime is variable and depends on the used technology; for examples membranes need to be replaced every 2-3 years.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Desalination is the process of removing salts and minerals from seawater or brackish water, transforming it into freshwater suitable for drinking, agriculture, and industry.
Desalination addresses global water scarcity, providing a reliable source of freshwater for over 300 million people, particularly in arid regions facing climate challenges.
Modern desalination technologies, including reverse osmosis and solar desalination, enhance efficiency, reduce costs, and lower environmental impacts, making water production more sustainable.
